VPC & Networking Foundation
description:
## Summary
Provision the foundational AWS VPC and networking infrastructure that all other AWS resources depend on.

## Region & CIDR Blocks
- **Region**: us-east-2
- **Staging VPC**: 10.0.0.0/16
- **Prod VPC**: 10.1.0.0/16

Non-overlapping CIDRs allow future VPC peering if needed.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- VPC with public/private/database subnets across 2-3 AZs
- fck-nat instance for private subnet egress (cost-optimized)
- Internet gateway for public subnets
- Route tables (public, private, database)
- Security groups (baseline, can be extended by dependent epics)
- S3 Gateway Endpoint (free) for ECR layers and logs

## Environment Strategy
Two AWS environments, both in the same account (can split later):

| Environment | Purpose | VPC CIDR |
|-------------|---------|----------|
| Staging | CI/CD, E2E tests, integration tests | 10.0.0.0/16 |
| Prod | Real users, isolated | 10.1.0.0/16 |

Local development uses Docker Compose (Postgres, Redis, mock auth) - no AWS resources.

The VPC module is instantiated twice with different tfvars (staging.tfvars, prod.tfvars).

## Subnet Tiers
| Tier | Purpose | Internet Access |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| Public | ALB, NAT | Direct (IGW) |
| Private | EKS pods, apps | Outbound only (NAT) |
| Database | RDS, Redis | None (isolated) |

## Key Decisions
- CIDR block sizing: /16 VPC with /20 subnets (4k IPs per subnet)
- AZ count: 2 for cost efficiency (can expand to 3 later)
- NAT: fck-nat on t4g.nano (~$3/mo) instead of NAT Gateway (~$32/mo)
- VPC Endpoints: S3 gateway only (free). Skip interface endpoints (~$7/mo each) - use fck-nat instead.
- DB access: Use kubectl exec into pods, no bastion needed
- Environments: Staging + Prod only. Local dev via Docker Compose.

## Deferred (add later if needed)
- **CloudFront**: Not needed initially. Easy to add in front of ALB later.
- **VPC Flow Logs**: Enable when debugging network issues. ~$0.50/GB to CloudWatch.
- **Route53**: DNS managed externally. ALB DNS name output is sufficient for CNAME setup.
- **Bastion/VPN**: Use kubectl exec or debug pods for DB access instead.
- **Tagging strategy**: Basic environment tags for now. Per-customer resource tagging will be important later for cost allocation.
- **Multi-account**: Prod can move to separate AWS account later if needed (~2 hours of work).

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/`
- Environment configs: staging.tfvars, prod.tfvars
- Outputs: VPC ID, subnet IDs (public, private, database), security group IDs

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for overall AWS topology and environment strategy
- fck-nat: https://fck-nat.dev/
close_reason: Obsolete: AWS VPC - project now uses GCP

title: Implement VPC with public/private/database subnets
description:
Create VPC with /16 CIDR block. Implement three subnet tiers across 2 AZs (expandable to 3):

- **Public subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): ALB, NAT instance
- **Private subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): EKS Fargate pods, application workloads
- **Database subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): RDS, ElastiCache - fully isolated, no NAT route

Database subnets have no route to internet (inbound or outbound). Only accessible from private subnets via security groups.

Use data source for available AZs in us-east-2.

**CIDR blocks** (from parent epic):
- Staging: 10.0.0.0/16
- Prod: 10.1.0.0/16

**Done when:**
- [ ] VPC created with /16 CIDR
- [ ] `enable_dns_hostnames = true` and `enable_dns_support = true` configured
- [ ] 2 public subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] 2 private subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] 2 database subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] `terraform plan` shows expected resources
- [ ] Subnets tagged with tier (public/private/database)
close_reason: Obsolete: AWS VPC tasks superseded by GCP migration (PLAT-vi8)

title: Implement Internet Gateway and NAT Gateways
description:
Create Internet Gateway for public subnets. Deploy fck-nat instance (t4g.nano) for NAT instead of NAT Gateway to minimize costs (~$3/mo vs ~$32/mo). Single NAT instance initially - can upgrade to NAT Gateway later if needed. Use Elastic IP for the NAT instance. Reference: https://fck-nat.dev/

**fck-nat configuration:**
- Instance type: t4g.nano (ARM, ~$3/mo)
- AMI: Use fck-nat community AMI for us-east-2
- Placement: First public subnet (us-east-2a)
- Elastic IP: Associate with ENI

**Critical**: The ENI must have `source_dest_check = false` to allow traffic to flow through the instance (hairpinning). Without this, NAT will not work.

**Done when:**
- [ ] Internet Gateway created and attached to VPC
- [ ] Elastic IP allocated for NAT
- [ ] fck-nat instance launched in public subnet with EIP
- [ ] ENI has `source_dest_check = false`
- [ ] NAT instance can reach internet (test: curl from instance)
close_reason: Obsolete: AWS VPC tasks superseded by GCP migration (PLAT-vi8)

title: Implement route tables for public and private subnets
description:
Create route tables for each subnet tier:

- **Public route table**: Default route (0.0.0.0/0) to Internet Gateway. Associate with public subnets.
- **Private route table**: Default route to fck-nat instance. Associate with private subnets.
- **Database route table**: No default route (fully isolated). Associate with database subnets.

Database tier has no outbound internet path - RDS/Redis can only be reached from within the VPC.

**Done when:**
- [ ] Public route table with 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW
- [ ] Private route table with 0.0.0.0/0 → fck-nat ENI
- [ ] Database route table with local routes only
- [ ] Each subnet associated with correct route table
close_reason: Obsolete: AWS VPC tasks superseded by GCP migration (PLAT-vi8)

title: Implement baseline security groups (Fargate model)
description:
Create baseline security groups for Fargate-based EKS (no EC2 nodes):

- **ALB SG**: Inbound 80/443 from 0.0.0.0/0, outbound to EKS Pod SG
- **EKS Pod SG**: Inbound from ALB SG on app ports, outbound to RDS SG, Redis SG, and 0.0.0.0/0 (internet via NAT). Attached to Fargate pod ENIs via EKS SecurityGroupPolicy.
- **RDS SG**: Inbound 5432 from EKS Pod SG only
- **Redis SG**: Inbound 6379 from EKS Pod SG only
- **NAT SG**: Inbound all traffic from VPC CIDR, outbound all traffic to 0.0.0.0/0. Per fck-nat docs, traffic flows THROUGH the instance (hairpinning), not TO it.

Note: Fargate pods get individual ENIs with VPC IPs - no "node" security group needed. The EKS Pod SG is applied to pod ENIs, not EC2 instances.

**Important**: The fck-nat ENI must have `source_dest_check = false` to allow pass-through traffic. This is configured in PLAT-03e.3 (NAT instance task).

**Done when:**
- [ ] All 5 security groups created
- [ ] NAT SG allows all traffic from VPC CIDR inbound
- [ ] Rules reference other SGs by ID (not CIDR) where applicable
- [ ] Security group IDs exported as outputs

title: Implement S3 Gateway Endpoint
description:
Create S3 Gateway Endpoint (free) for ECR image layers, logs, and Terraform state. Skip interface endpoints (ECR API, CloudWatch, STS, Secrets Manager) - let this traffic go through fck-nat instead. Interface endpoints cost ~$7/mo/AZ each which defeats the cost savings from fck-nat.

Future consideration: Add interface endpoints if fck-nat becomes a bottleneck or if security requirements mandate traffic stay within VPC.

**Done when:**
- [ ] S3 Gateway Endpoint created
- [ ] Endpoint associated with private and database route tables
- [ ] S3 traffic from private subnets does not go through NAT (verify in route table)

title: Bootstrap Terraform state backend
description:
Create S3 bucket and DynamoDB table for Terraform remote state before any other infrastructure.

**Resources:**
- S3 bucket: `eval-terraform-state` with versioning enabled
- DynamoDB table: `terraform-locks` with PAY_PER_REQUEST billing, LockID (string) as hash key

**Bootstrap process:**
1. Create `infrastructure/terraform/bootstrap/` with state backend resources
2. Apply locally (state stored in git initially, one-time only)
3. Configure backend in `infrastructure/terraform/environments/` to use remote state
4. All subsequent terraform runs use S3 + DynamoDB

**S3 bucket settings:**
- Versioning: enabled (recover from bad applies)
- Encryption: AES-256 (SSE-S3)
- Public access: blocked
- Lifecycle: keep all versions (state history)

**Done when:**
- [ ] S3 bucket `eval-terraform-state` exists with versioning enabled
- [ ] DynamoDB table `terraform-locks` exists with LockID key
- [ ] `terraform init` in environments/ configures remote backend successfully
- [ ] `terraform plan` acquires and releases lock
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed - Bootstrap module complete with S3 (AES256 encryption, versioning, public access blocked) and DynamoDB (terraform-locks, PAY_PER_REQUEST). terraform validate passes. Actual apply requires AWS credentials.

title: CI failures on PR #31: contract tests, frontend tests, and store integration tests
description:
## Summary
PR #31 (feat: frontend-to-API contract test harness) had 3 CI failures. All fixed.

## Status

### 1. Contract Tests - FIXED ✓
- **Root cause**: System-admin users have no namespace, so couldn't create classes (RLS policy violation).
- **Fix**: Split auth middleware (PLAT-loq), contract tests now create namespace + instructor user via invitation flow before creating classes.

### 2. Frontend Tests - FIXED ✓
- Frontend unit tests all pass (128 suites, 1820 tests)
- Contract tests separated into their own npm script `test:contract`

### 3. Store Integration Tests - FIXED ✓
- **Root cause**: Test isolation issues - leftover data from other test runs caused:
  - Join code unique constraint violations
  - Count assertions failing due to extra records
- **Fix**: 
  - createSection helper now appends unique suffix to join codes
  - ListProblemsFiltered/ListSessions filter by namespace
  - Count assertions changed to verify presence of created records

## PR Reference
- PR #31: https://github.com/jdelfino/eval/pull/31
- Branch: `feature/contract-test-harness`
close_reason: All CI failures fixed: contract tests via auth middleware split, store integration tests via proper test isolation

Port practice mode and session-lifecycle E2E test from coding-tool
description:
## Summary

Port the practice mode feature (allowing students to run code after sessions end) and the new session-lifecycle E2E test from coding-tool. Port up to: 78067cb.

## Origin

coding-tool commits: dbaf12a (#59), 78067cb. These are the most recent functional commits since the last sync.

## Part 1: Practice mode for completed sessions

In coding-tool, practice mode has 3 parts:
1. **Backend: practice execution endpoint** - `POST /api/sessions/[id]/practice` with rate limiting
2. **Backend: allow code saving in completed sessions** - removes the completed-session block from code save
3. **Frontend: student page UI** - enables run button + editing in ended sessions, calls practice endpoint

### Eval implementation approach

The backend is different (Go, not Node API routes), so this needs:

**Go backend:**
- Add `POST /api/v1/sessions/{id}/practice` handler that runs code via the executor service
- Rate limit: 15 req/min per user (use existing rate limiter or add one)
- Auth: verify user is a participant in the session
- Remove or relax the 'session must be active' check in the code save handler (if it exists)

**Frontend (`frontend/src/app/(fullscreen)/student/page.tsx`):**
- Add `handlePracticeRun` that POSTs to the practice endpoint directly (bypasses realtime)
- In `handleRunCode`, delegate to `handlePracticeRun` when `sessionEnded` is true
- Remove `sessionEnded` from debounced code save dependency (allow saving in practice mode)
- Change CodeEditor props: `readOnly={false}`, `showRunButton={true}`, `onRun={handleRunCode}`, `onLoadStarterCode={handleLoadStarterCode}` (always enabled, not gated on sessionEnded)

## Part 2: Session-lifecycle E2E test

Port `e2e/session-lifecycle.spec.ts` from coding-tool. The test covers:
1. Instructor starts session from problem page (InstructorActions)
2. Student joins and types code
3. Instructor starts new session from different problem (session replacement)
4. Student sees replacement notification and joins new session
5. Student types code in new session, instructor verifies
6. Instructor ends session
7. Student runs code in practice mode

### Eval adaptation needed

- Replace Supabase Admin setup (`getSupabaseAdmin`, `setupTestNamespaceWithSection`, `createInstructorForSection`) with eval's API helpers (`testNamespace` fixture, `setupInstructor`, `apiFetch`)
- Add `createProblem` helper to `e2e/fixtures/api-setup.ts`: POST to `/api/v1/problems`
- Add `registerStudent` flow using eval's existing helper
- Replace `loginAsInstructor`/`loginAsStudent` with eval's `signInAs`
- Replace `cleanupNamespace` with eval's fixture-based cleanup
- Use eval's `logCollector` fixture for debug output
- Adapt URL patterns: `?sessionId=` vs `?session_id=` (check eval's conventions)

## Testing

The E2E test itself IS the test. Run with `make e2e` or equivalent.

title: Add store layer integration tests (handler→store→PostgreSQL)
description:
## Summary

The current store integration tests (`store_*_integration_test.go`) don't actually test the Store methods. They copy-paste SQL queries and run them directly against a superuser connection, bypassing RLS entirely. This provides false confidence - tests pass but don't reflect production behavior.

## Problems with Current Tests

1. **Tests copy-paste SQL instead of calling Store methods** - Changes to actual Store methods won't be caught
2. **No RLS context set** - Tests run as superuser, bypassing all row-level security policies
3. **Don't instantiate Store type** - The `store.New()` constructor is never called
4. **Namespace isolation not tested** - A bug allowing cross-namespace data leaks would pass tests

## Example of Current (Broken) Pattern

```go
// Current: copies SQL from problems.go, runs directly
runFiltered := func(t *testing.T, filters ProblemFilters) []Problem {
    query := `SELECT ... FROM problems WHERE 1=1`  // <-- copied SQL, not real code
    rows, err := conn.Query(ctx, query, args...)   // <-- superuser connection
    // ...
}
```

## Required Fix

Tests should call actual Store methods with proper RLS context:

```go
// Acquire connection and set RLS context (like production middleware)
conn, _ := pool.Acquire(ctx)
defer conn.Release()

conn.Exec(ctx, "SET ROLE eval_app")
conn.Exec(ctx, "SELECT set_config('app.user_id', $1, true)", userID)
conn.Exec(ctx, "SELECT set_config('app.namespace_id', $1, true)", nsID)
conn.Exec(ctx, "SELECT set_config('app.role', $1, true)", "instructor")

// Create Store and call ACTUAL methods
s := store.New(conn)
problems, err := s.ListProblems(ctx, nil)  // <-- tests real code + RLS
```

## Files to Modify

- `go-backend/internal/store/store_methods_integration_test.go`
- `go-backend/internal/store/store_session_integration_test.go`
- `go-backend/internal/store/store_crud_integration_test.go`

## Implementation Steps

1. Add helper to set up RLS context (reuse pattern from `integration_test.go:setRLSContext`)
2. Rewrite each test to:
   - Acquire connection from pool
   - Set RLS context for test user
   - Call actual Store methods via `store.New(conn)`
   - Verify results
3. Add cross-namespace isolation tests (user in ns-A queries, verify ns-B data not returned)
4. Remove all copy-pasted SQL from test files

title: Identity Platform SAML Integration
description:
## Summary
Configure Identity Platform for SAML-based authentication with enterprise identity providers (university SSO systems).

## Scope
- SAML Identity Provider configuration in Identity Platform
- Attribute mapping from SAML assertions
- Testing with a mock SAML IdP

## SAML Configuration (Manual in GCP Console)
1. Go to: https://console.cloud.google.com/customer-identity/providers
2. Click "Add a Provider" > "SAML"
3. Configure:
   - Provider name
   - IdP Entity ID
   - SSO URL
   - X.509 Certificate
4. Note the SP Entity ID and ACS URL for your IdP

## SAML Attribute Mapping Strategy
Map standard attributes:
- `email` → email
- `given_name` → first name
- `family_name` → last name

**IdP-specific attributes** (eduPersonAffiliation, eppn, department, etc.) are captured by the Go API on first login and stored in the application database per-tenant.

## Flows to Support
1. SP-initiated SSO (user clicks "Login with SSO" → redirect to IdP)
2. IdP-initiated SSO (user starts at IdP, lands at app)

## Deliverables
- SAML provider configured in Identity Platform
- Documentation on claim passthrough
- Mock SAML IdP setup for testing (e.g., samlidp, Keycloak)
- Integration test scripts

## Dependencies
- Requires: Identity Platform base setup (PLAT-dar)

## References
- GCP Identity Platform SAML docs
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for auth flow overview

Add concurrency limit to executor service
description:
## Summary
The executor service has no concurrency limit on nsjail process spawning. A burst of requests can exhaust pod resources before KEDA scales up.

## Files to modify
- `executor/internal/handler/execute.go`
- `executor/internal/config/config.go`

## Implementation
1. Add MaxConcurrentExecutions config field (default: e.g., 10)
2. Add a semaphore (chan struct{} or golang.org/x/sync/semaphore) in the handler
3. Return 429 Too Many Requests when semaphore is full
4. Add tests for concurrency limiting behavior

title: Add practice mode for completed sessions
description:
## Summary

Allow students to continue editing and running code after a session ends. Practice mode uses ephemeral execution (no persistent state) with rate limiting.

## Origin

coding-tool commit dbaf12a (#59). Port up to: 78067cb.

## Backend (Go)

**New handler:** `go-backend/internal/handler/practice.go` (or add to sessions handler)
- `POST /api/v1/sessions/{id}/practice` - executes code via executor service
- Auth: verify user is a participant in the session
- Rate limit: 15 req/min per user
- Request body: `{ code, execution_settings }`
- Response: execution result (stdout, stderr, exit_code, etc.)

**Modify:** Remove or relax 'session must be active' check in the code save handler if it exists, so students can persist practice edits.

## Frontend

**File:** `frontend/src/app/(fullscreen)/student/page.tsx`

1. Add `handlePracticeRun` function that POSTs to `/api/v1/sessions/${sessionId}/practice`
2. In `handleRunCode`, delegate to `handlePracticeRun` when `sessionEnded` is true
3. Remove `sessionEnded` gate from debounced code save (allow saving in practice)
4. Change CodeEditor props to always enable interaction:
   - `readOnly={false}` (was `readOnly={sessionEnded}`)
   - `showRunButton={true}` (was `showRunButton={\!sessionEnded}`)
   - `onRun={handleRunCode}` (was `onRun={sessionEnded ? undefined : handleRunCode}`)
   - `onLoadStarterCode={handleLoadStarterCode}` (was gated on sessionEnded)

**Tests:** `frontend/src/app/(fullscreen)/student/__tests__/page-session-ended.test.tsx` - add practice run tests
close_reason: Added PracticeExecute handler with rate limiting (15/min), practice API client, updated student page for always-editable practice mode. 7 Go tests + 13 frontend tests pass.

title: DeleteMembershipIfNotLast uses FOR UPDATE with aggregate — incompatible with PG 15+
description:
SELECT COUNT(*) ... FOR UPDATE is not valid in PostgreSQL 15+. Aggregates cannot use FOR UPDATE. Discovered by integration tests in 19c5304.

Files: go-backend/internal/store/memberships.go (DeleteMembershipIfNotLast)

Fix: Use a subquery or SELECT ... FOR UPDATE without the aggregate, then count in application code. Or use advisory locks.

title: RBAC permission system with RequirePermission middleware
description:
Current RequireRole() middleware is brittle — adding a grader role requires updating every route. Replace with permission-based RBAC.

Simplified permission set (~10 capabilities vs coding-tool's 57):
- session.join, session.manage
- content.manage (CRUD classes/sections/problems)
- data.viewOwn, data.viewAll, data.export
- user.manage, user.changeRole
- namespace.manage, system.admin

Role→permission mapping in a single Go map. Middleware: RequirePermission("content.manage").

Files:
- go-backend/internal/auth/permissions.go — permission constants + role map
- go-backend/internal/middleware/authz.go — add RequirePermission() alongside existing RequireRole()
- Migrate existing RequireRole() calls to RequirePermission() in route definitions

title: Database Schema & Row-Level Security
description:
## Summary
Design and implement the PostgreSQL database schema with Row-Level Security (RLS) policies for multi-tenant isolation.

## Scope
- Complete database schema (tables, indexes, constraints)
- RLS policies using PostgreSQL session variables
- Database migrations tooling (golang-migrate or similar)
- Seed data for development/testing

## Core Tables (based on existing app)
- `namespaces` - multi-tenant organizations
- `users` - user accounts (linked to Cognito)
- `classes` - courses
- `sections` - class sections
- `problems` - assignment problems
- `sessions` - live coding sessions
- `invitations` - user invitations

## RLS Implementation
```sql
-- Example RLS policy pattern
ALTER TABLE classes ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON classes
  USING (namespace_id = current_setting('app.namespace_id', true)::uuid);
```

Session variables set by middleware:
- `app.user_id` - current user UUID
- `app.namespace_id` - current tenant UUID
- `app.role` - user role for permission checks

## Deliverables
- SQL migration files: `go-backend/migrations/`
- Schema documentation
- RLS policy test suite
- Development seed data script

## Dependencies
- Requires: RDS PostgreSQL Database (PLAT-etu)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 2: Auth + Database Schema
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for RLS middleware pattern

title: Unify local and CI build tooling (eliminate Make/CI mismatch)
description:
## Summary
The Makefile is used for some local tasks (build, test, lint, docker-build) but CI workflows duplicate the same commands in GitHub Actions YAML. This creates a maintenance burden where changes must be made in two places, and CI behavior can drift from local behavior.

## Problem
Examples of duplication:
- `make test-api` runs `go test -race ./...` — go-api.yaml also runs `go test -v -race ./...` (different flags)
- `make lint-api` runs `golangci-lint run ./...` — go-api.yaml uses `golangci-lint-action@v4`
- `make docker-build-api` builds with `go-backend/` as context — go-api.yaml builds with `.` as context and `-f go-backend/Dockerfile`
- Frontend CI has no `docker-build-frontend` make target, while go-api and executor do

The Makefile is incomplete (missing `docker-build-frontend`) and CI doesn't use it, so adding to one doesn't fix the other.

## Options to evaluate
1. **Make CI use Makefile targets** — CI steps become `make test-api`, `make lint-api`, etc. Single source of truth. But some CI-specific needs (caching, actions) may not fit.
2. **Remove Makefile, use scripts/** — Each task gets a script (`scripts/test-api.sh`), CI and local both call it. More explicit.
3. **Use a task runner** (just, taskfile, etc.) — Modern alternative to Make with better cross-platform support.
4. **Accept the duplication** — Document it and move on. Pragmatic but accumulates drift.

## Action items
- Decide on approach
- Implement chosen solution
- Ensure `docker-build-frontend` works identically locally and in CI

title: Cloud DNS + Managed SSL + HTTPS for eval.delquillan.com
Create dns-ssl Terraform module
description:
## Summary
Create a new Terraform module that provisions Cloud DNS and a static global IP for the ingress load balancer. The SSL certificate is handled via GKE ManagedCertificate CR (in task 3), not Terraform.

## Files to create
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/dns-ssl/variables.tf`
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/dns-ssl/main.tf`
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/dns-ssl/outputs.tf`
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/dns-ssl/versions.tf`

## Implementation steps
1. Create `versions.tf` following the pattern from existing modules (e.g., `modules/artifact-registry/versions.tf`)
2. Create `variables.tf` with: environment, project_name, project_id, region (common vars), domain_name (string, e.g. "eval.delquillan.com")
3. Create `main.tf` with these resources:
   - `google_project_service` to enable `dns.googleapis.com`
   - `google_compute_global_address` named `"${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-ingress-ip"` for static LB IP
   - `google_dns_managed_zone` for the domain (dns_name = "${var.domain_name}.", visibility = "public")
   - `google_dns_record_set` A record pointing domain to the static IP (TTL 300)
   - Do NOT create google_compute_managed_ssl_certificate — SSL is handled via GKE ManagedCertificate CR in k8s manifests
4. Create `outputs.tf` exposing:
   - `static_ip_name` — for ingress annotation
   - `static_ip_address` — the actual IP
   - `dns_name_servers` — the zone's NS records (for GoDaddy delegation)
   - `godaddy_instructions` — heredoc with step-by-step instructions to add NS records for the `eval` subdomain in GoDaddy's DNS management (NOT changing the domain's nameservers — adding NS records for the subdomain)
5. Run `terraform fmt` and `terraform validate` (with init -backend=false) on the module

title: Wire dns-ssl module into prod environment
description:
## Summary
Instantiate the dns-ssl module in the prod environment config and expose its outputs.

## Files to modify
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/variables.tf` — add domain_name variable
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/terraform.tfvars` — set domain_name = "eval.delquillan.com"
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/main.tf` — add module "dns_ssl" block
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/outputs.tf` — expose nameservers and instructions

## Implementation steps
1. Add to `variables.tf`:
   ```hcl
   variable "domain_name" {
     description = "Domain name for the application (e.g., eval.delquillan.com)"
     type        = string
   }
   ```
2. Add to `terraform.tfvars`:
   ```hcl
   domain_name = "eval.delquillan.com"
   ```
3. Add to `main.tf` (after the monitoring module block):
   ```hcl
   module "dns_ssl" {
     source = "../../modules/dns-ssl"
     environment  = var.environment
     project_name = var.project_name
     project_id   = var.project_id
     region       = var.region
     domain_name  = var.domain_name
   }
   ```
4. Add to `outputs.tf`:
   ```hcl
   output "dns_name_servers" {
     description = "Cloud DNS nameservers — add these as NS records in GoDaddy"
     value       = module.dns_ssl.dns_name_servers
   }
   output "ingress_static_ip" {
     description = "Static IP address for the ingress load balancer"
     value       = module.dns_ssl.static_ip_address
   }
   output "godaddy_instructions" {
     description = "Instructions for GoDaddy DNS delegation"
     value       = module.dns_ssl.godaddy_instructions
   }
   ```
5. Run `terraform fmt` on modified files

title: Update ingress for HTTPS with managed cert and HTTP redirect
description:
## Summary
Update the Kubernetes ingress (now in consolidated k8s/base/) to use the static IP, GKE ManagedCertificate, and add HTTP→HTTPS redirect via FrontendConfig.

## Prerequisite
PLAT-4yz.4 (k8s directory consolidation) must be done first — ingress.yaml will already be in k8s/base/.
Terraform must be applied (PLAT-4yz.1 + PLAT-4yz.2) so the static IP exists before the ingress references it.

## Files to modify
- `k8s/base/ingress.yaml` — add SSL/static-IP annotations

## Files to create
- `k8s/base/frontend-config.yaml` — FrontendConfig for HTTP→HTTPS redirect
- `k8s/base/managed-certificate.yaml` — GKE ManagedCertificate CR for eval.delquillan.com

## Files to update
- `k8s/base/kustomization.yaml` — add new resources

## Implementation steps
1. Update `k8s/base/ingress.yaml` annotations:
   ```yaml
   metadata:
     name: app-ingress
     annotations:
       kubernetes.io/ingress.class: gce
       kubernetes.io/ingress.global-static-ip-name: eval-prod-ingress-ip
       networking.gke.io/managed-certificates: eval-delquillan-com
       networking.gke.io/v1beta1.FrontendConfig: https-redirect
   ```

2. Create `k8s/base/frontend-config.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   apiVersion: networking.gke.io/v1beta1
   kind: FrontendConfig
   metadata:
     name: https-redirect
   spec:
     redirectToHttps:
       enabled: true
       responseCodeName: MOVED_PERMANENTLY_DEFAULT
   ```

3. Create `k8s/base/managed-certificate.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   apiVersion: networking.gke.io/v1
   kind: ManagedCertificate
   metadata:
     name: eval-delquillan-com
   spec:
     domains:
       - eval.delquillan.com
   ```

4. Add to `k8s/base/kustomization.yaml` resources:
   ```yaml
   - frontend-config.yaml
   - managed-certificate.yaml
   ```

5. Validate: `kustomize build k8s/base/`

title: Consolidate k8s directories: merge infrastructure/k8s/base into k8s/base
description:
## Summary
Merge the two diverged k8s directories into the single canonical `k8s/base/` directory used by the deploy pipeline. Apply production hardening from the infrastructure versions (security contexts, resource limits) but keep replicas at 1 for cost. Delete `infrastructure/k8s/` entirely when done.

## Current state
- `k8s/base/` — active (deploy pipeline), has: go-api, executor, redis, frontend, monitoring
- `infrastructure/k8s/base/` — stale, has: ingress, serviceaccount, centrifugo, production-hardened deployments
- `infrastructure/k8s/centrifugo/config.json` — dead template file (prod config is created by Terraform centrifugo module)

## Files to move INTO k8s/base/ (from infrastructure/k8s/base/)
1. `ingress.yaml` — path-based routing (copy as-is)
2. `serviceaccount.yaml` — go-api ServiceAccount (copy as-is)
3. `centrifugo-deployment.yaml` — copy but set replicas: 1 (infrastructure version has 2)
4. `centrifugo-service.yaml` — copy as-is
5. `centrifugo-backend-config.yaml` — GCP backend timeout for WebSocket (copy as-is)

## Files to harden IN k8s/base/ (adopt security from infrastructure versions)

   - `spec.template.spec.serviceAccountName: go-api`
   - `spec.template.spec.containers[0].resources.limits` (500m CPU, 512Mi memory)
   - `spec.template.spec.containers[0].securityContext`: runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 65534, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
   - Keep existing resource requests (250m CPU, 256Mi memory) — do NOT adopt the lower infra values
   - Keep replicas: 1

7. `service.yaml` (go-api) — add `name: http` to port

8. `frontend-deployment.yaml` — add from infrastructure version:

   - `spec.template.spec.containers[0].securityContext`: runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1001, readOnlyRootFilesystem: false
   - Named port: `name: http` on containerPort 3000

title: Go API Project Scaffolding
description:
## Summary
Establish the Go API project structure, configuration management, and build/deployment pipeline. Uses Chi router with go-playground/validator.

## Scope
- Project directory structure per `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- Chi router setup with middleware chain
- Configuration management (env vars, feature flags)
- Request binding and validation helpers using go-playground/validator
- Dockerfile and container build
- Kubernetes deployment manifests
- CI/CD pipeline configuration

## Project Structure
\`\`\`
go-backend/
├── cmd/server/main.go       # Entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── config/              # Configuration loading
│   ├── server/              # HTTP server setup, Chi router
│   ├── middleware/          # Logging, recovery, request ID
│   └── handler/             # HTTP handlers (initially just health)
├── pkg/                     # Shared libraries
├── migrations/              # SQL migrations
├── Dockerfile
└── go.mod
\`\`\`

## Framework Choice: Chi
- Minimal, stdlib-compatible router
- No framework lock-in (standard http.Handler interface)
- Explicit middleware composition

## Validation Helper Pattern
\`\`\`go
var validate = validator.New()

func BindJSON[T any](r *http.Request) (T, error) {
    var req T
    if err := json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req); err != nil {
        return req, err
    }
    return req, validate.Struct(req)
}
\`\`\`

## Deliverables
- Complete project skeleton with working health endpoint
- Docker build and local run instructions
- Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, Ingress)
- CI pipeline (build, test, push to ECR)
- Makefile for common tasks

## Dependencies
- Requires: EKS Compute Platform (PLAT-b3l) - for deployment target

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for Go API structure
- Chi router: github.com/go-chi/chi/v5
- Validator: github.com/go-playground/validator/v10
close_reason: Merged: feat: Go API Project Scaffolding (#4)

title: Replace CI env var workaround with dedicated E2E_REUSE_SERVERS variable
description:
## Summary
The E2E CI workflow sets CI='' to make Playwright's reuseExistingServer: \!process.env.CI evaluate to true. This is fragile because other tools (Next.js, npm) also read CI to adjust behavior.

## Implementation
- Add a dedicated env var (e.g., E2E_REUSE_SERVERS) in playwright.config.ts:
  reuseExistingServer: \!\!process.env.E2E_REUSE_SERVERS || \!process.env.CI
- In the CI workflow, set E2E_REUSE_SERVERS=true instead of CI=''
- This preserves CI=true for other tools while still reusing pre-started servers
- The --forbid-only CLI flag can be kept as defense-in-depth

## Files
- frontend/playwright.config.ts
- .github/workflows/e2e-tests.yml

title: Frontend CD: deploy Next.js to GKE
description:
## Summary
Deploy the Next.js frontend to GKE as a separate Deployment/Service in the same cluster and namespace as the Go backend. Path-based Ingress routing: `/api/*` → go-api, `/*` → frontend.

## Key Decisions
- Separate GKE Deployment/Service (not sidecar or Cloud Run)
- Same domain, path-based routing via GCE Ingress
- Runtime env substitution: generic Docker image with placeholder NEXT_PUBLIC_* vars, sed-replaced at container start via entrypoint script
- This means readOnlyRootFilesystem must be false for the frontend container (entrypoint modifies built JS files)

## Scope
- Frontend Docker image (multi-stage build, standalone output, entrypoint script)
- Dev K8s manifests (k8s/base/)
- Prod K8s manifests + Ingress routing (infrastructure/k8s/base/)
- CI workflow (GitHub Actions: lint → test → build-push)
- Deploy workflow update (trigger on Frontend CI completion)
- Terraform ConfigMap for prod env vars

title: Frontend Docker image (Dockerfile, entrypoint, .dockerignore)
description:
## Summary
Create the Docker build for the Next.js frontend: multi-stage Dockerfile, env-substitution entrypoint, and .dockerignore. Also add `output: 'standalone'` to next.config.js (required for standalone build).

## Files
- `frontend/Dockerfile` (new)
- `frontend/docker-entrypoint.sh` (new)
- `frontend/.dockerignore` (new)
- `frontend/next.config.js` (modify — add `output: 'standalone'`)

## Dockerfile (multi-stage)
- **deps stage**: `node:22-alpine`, copy package*.json, `npm ci`
- **builder stage**: copy deps, copy source, build with placeholder env vars (`__NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL__` etc. as build-args or hardcoded), `npm run build`
- **runner stage**: `node:22-alpine`, copy `.next/standalone`, `.next/static`, `public` (with `--chown=nextjs:nodejs`), copy entrypoint, user `nextjs` (UID 1001), expose 3000, ENTRYPOINT entrypoint script, CMD `node server.js`

Build context is repo root to match CI pattern: `docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile .`

## Entrypoint — runtime env substitution
The entrypoint script (`docker-entrypoint.sh`) uses `find + sed` to replace `__NEXT_PUBLIC_*__` placeholder strings in built `.js` and `.html` files under `/app/.next/` with actual env var values at container start. Finishes with `exec "$@"` to run CMD.

This requires `readOnlyRootFilesystem: false` in K8s (accepted tradeoff — all other security hardening preserved).

## .dockerignore
Exclude: `node_modules`, `.next`, `.env*`, `*.test.*`, `playwright*`, `coverage`, `.git`

## next.config.js change
Add `output: 'standalone'` to the config object. This tells Next.js to produce a self-contained server in `.next/standalone/`.

## Verification
- `docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile -t frontend:local .` succeeds
- `docker run -e NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=http://localhost:8080/api/v1 -p 3000:3000 frontend:local` — app serves on port 3000

title: Dev K8s manifests for frontend
description:
## Summary
Add frontend Deployment, Service, and ConfigMap to the dev K8s manifests (`k8s/base/`), and register them in kustomization.yaml.

## Files
- `k8s/base/frontend-deployment.yaml` (new)
- `k8s/base/frontend-service.yaml` (new)
- `k8s/base/frontend-configmap.yaml` (new)
- `k8s/base/kustomization.yaml` (modify)

## frontend-deployment.yaml
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: frontend
  labels:
    app: frontend
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: frontend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: frontend
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: frontend
          image: frontend
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: frontend-config
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /
              port: 3000
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 15
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /
              port: 3000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
```

## frontend-service.yaml
ClusterIP service, port 80 → targetPort 3000, selector `app: frontend`.

## frontend-configmap.yaml
ConfigMap `frontend-config` with dev placeholder values:
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL`: `/api/v1`
- Other `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` vars as needed (depends on PLAT-663.1 entrypoint approach)

## kustomization.yaml changes
Add to `resources`: `frontend-deployment.yaml`, `frontend-service.yaml`, `frontend-configmap.yaml`
Add to `images`: `- name: frontend` / `newName: frontend`

## Verification
`kustomize build k8s/base > /dev/null` validates without errors

title: Prod K8s manifests and Ingress routing for frontend
description:
## Summary
Add frontend Deployment and Service to prod K8s manifests (`infrastructure/k8s/base/`), update the Ingress for path-based routing, and fix the kustomization.yaml labels issue.

## Files
- `infrastructure/k8s/base/frontend-deployment.yaml` (new)
- `infrastructure/k8s/base/frontend-service.yaml` (new)
- `infrastructure/k8s/base/kustomization.yaml` (modify)
- `infrastructure/k8s/base/ingress.yaml` (modify)

## frontend-deployment.yaml (prod)
Similar to dev but with prod hardening:
- 2 replicas
- Full security context: runAsNonRoot, runAsUser 1001, drop ALL capabilities, no privilege escalation
- `readOnlyRootFilesystem: false` (required for entrypoint sed substitution of NEXT_PUBLIC_* placeholders at startup)
- emptyDir volume at `/tmp` for Next.js cache
- Resource limits: 128Mi-256Mi memory, 100m-500m CPU
- envFrom: `frontend-config` ConfigMap (created by Terraform, see PLAT-663.6)

## frontend-service.yaml (prod)
Same pattern as go-api service: ClusterIP, port 80 → targetPort 3000, port name `http`.

## kustomization.yaml fix
**Current problem**: The `labels` block applies `app.kubernetes.io/name: go-api` with `includeSelectors: true` to ALL resources. Adding a frontend Deployment would corrupt its selectors (frontend pods would get go-api selector labels).

**Fix**: Change `includeSelectors: false` and remove `app.kubernetes.io/name: go-api` from the global labels (each resource already has its own `app: <name>` label via their own metadata). Keep `app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: kustomize` as a global non-selector label.

Add frontend resources to the resources list.

## ingress.yaml — path-based routing
Convert from single-backend to path-based routing:

**Before**: Single `defaultBackend` and single `/*` rule both pointing to go-api.

**After**:
- `defaultBackend` → frontend service (catch-all for unmatched paths)
- Rules:
  - `/api/*` → go-api:80 (pathType: ImplementationSpecific)
  - `/*` → frontend:80 (pathType: ImplementationSpecific)

The Ingress name can change (no need to preserve for IP stability).

## Verification
- `kustomize build infrastructure/k8s/base > /dev/null` validates
- Rendered output shows correct selectors (frontend gets `app: frontend`, go-api gets `app: go-api`)
- Ingress has correct path routing rules

title: Add Docker build-push job to frontend CI workflow
description:
## Summary
Add a `build-push` job to the existing frontend CI workflow (`.github/workflows/frontend-ci.yml`) that builds the Docker image and pushes it to Artifact Registry on main branch pushes.

## Files
- `.github/workflows/frontend-ci.yml` (modify)

## Current state
The workflow already has `typecheck`, `test`, and `lint` jobs. It triggers on pushes to main (paths: `frontend/**`) and PRs.

## Changes
Add a `build-push` job that:
1. Depends on `typecheck`, `test`, `lint` (all must pass)
2. Only runs on push to main (`if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'`)
3. Uses WIF auth (same pattern as go-api.yaml):
   ```yaml
   permissions:
     contents: read
     id-token: write
   steps:
     - uses: actions/checkout@v4
     - uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
       with:
         workload_identity_provider: ${{ secrets.WIF_PROVIDER }}
         service_account: ${{ secrets.WIF_SERVICE_ACCOUNT }}
     - run: gcloud auth configure-docker us-east1-docker.pkg.dev
     - run: |
         docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile -t $REGISTRY/frontend:${{ github.sha }} .
         docker push $REGISTRY/frontend:${{ github.sha }}
   ```
4. Add `REGISTRY: us-east1-docker.pkg.dev/eval-prod-485520/go-api` to env block

Also update the workflow `name` to `"Frontend"` (currently `"Frontend CI"`) so the deploy workflow trigger matches (see PLAT-663.5). Or match whatever name the deploy workflow uses.

## Verification
- Workflow YAML is valid
- Push to main touching `frontend/` triggers lint + test + build-push
- PR only triggers lint + test (no build-push)

title: Update deploy workflow to handle frontend CI trigger
description:
## Summary
Update `.github/workflows/deploy.yaml` to trigger on frontend CI completion and deploy the frontend image alongside go-api and executor.

## Files
- `.github/workflows/deploy.yaml` (modify)

## Current state
The deploy workflow triggers on completion of "Go API" and "Executor" workflows. It reads current live images for both services, then overrides whichever triggered the deploy while preserving the other.

## Changes

1. **Add trigger**: Add the frontend CI workflow name to `workflow_run.workflows` list:
   ```yaml
   workflows: ["Go API", "Executor", "Frontend"]  # or "Frontend CI" — must match the workflow name
   ```

2. **Read current frontend image**: Add alongside existing image reads:
   ```bash
   FE_IMG=$(kubectl get deploy frontend -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}' 2>/dev/null || echo "")
   ```

3. **Add frontend case**: Add `elif` for frontend trigger:
   ```bash
   elif [ "$TRIGGER" = "Frontend" ]; then
     kustomize edit set image "frontend=$REGISTRY/frontend:$SHA"
     [ -n "$API_IMG" ] && kustomize edit set image "go-api=$API_IMG"
     [ -n "$EXE_IMG" ] && kustomize edit set image "executor=$EXE_IMG"
   ```

4. **Preserve frontend in other cases**: Add `[ -n "$FE_IMG" ] && kustomize edit set image "frontend=$FE_IMG"` to the Go API, Executor, and manual dispatch branches.

## Verification
- Workflow YAML is valid
- Each trigger case preserves all other service images while updating the triggered one

title: Terraform ConfigMap for frontend prod env vars
description:
## Summary
Add a Terraform-managed `kubernetes_config_map` resource for the frontend's runtime environment variables in the prod environment. This ConfigMap is referenced by the frontend Deployment's `envFrom`.

## Files
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/main.tf` (modify — add `kubernetes_config_map.frontend_config`)
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/variables.tf` (modify — add `frontend_centrifugo_url` variable)
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/terraform.tfvars` (modify — add variable value)

## ConfigMap contents
```hcl
resource "kubernetes_config_map" "frontend_config" {
  metadata {
    name      = "frontend-config"
    namespace = var.k8s_namespace  # or whatever the existing namespace pattern is
  }

  data = {
    NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL           = "/api/v1"  # relative, same-origin via Ingress
    NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_API_KEY  = module.identity_platform.api_key  # or however Firebase config is exposed
    NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_AUTH_DOMAIN = module.identity_platform.auth_domain
    NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_PROJECT_ID = var.project_id
    NEXT_PUBLIC_CENTRIFUGO_URL    = var.frontend_centrifugo_url
  }
}
```

The exact attribute references depend on how the identity_platform module exports values — check during implementation.

**Note on Centrifugo URL**: See PLAT-??? (Centrifugo cluster-internal routing) — this should point to a cluster-internal address, not the public domain.

## Ordering
This ConfigMap must exist before the frontend Deployment is applied (Deployment references it via `envFrom`). Terraform apply must happen before K8s deploy.

## Verification
`cd infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod && terraform init -backend=false && terraform validate`

title: Migrate GKE from Autopilot to Standard with scale-to-zero
description: Switch from GKE Autopilot to a zonal GKE Standard cluster with two spot node pools (default + tainted executor), both min 0 for scale-to-zero during development. Replace Memorystore Redis with in-cluster Redis. Reduces idle cost from ~$125/mo to ~$35/mo while enabling executor's privileged capabilities (SYS_ADMIN, procMount: Unmasked) for nsjail sandboxing. Upgrade path to production is variable changes only (min counts, spot flags, zonal→regional).
close_reason: All subtasks complete. GKE migrated from Autopilot to Standard with scale-to-zero.

title: Convert GKE Terraform module from Autopilot to Standard with two node pools
description:
## Summary
Convert the GKE module from Autopilot mode to Standard mode with two configurable node pools (default + executor). The executor pool is tainted so only executor pods schedule there. Since Autopilot→Standard is not an in-place conversion, this will destroy and recreate the cluster (acceptable for pre-production).

## Files to modify
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/gke/main.tf`
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/gke/variables.tf`
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/gke/outputs.tf`

## Implementation steps
1. In main.tf, remove `enable_autopilot = true` from `google_container_cluster.autopilot`
2. Rename the resource from `google_container_cluster.autopilot` to `google_container_cluster.main` (update outputs accordingly). No `terraform state mv` needed — cluster will be destroyed and recreated since Autopilot→Standard is not an in-place operation.
3. Add `initial_node_count = 1` and `remove_default_node_pool = true` to the cluster resource (standard pattern for separately managed node pools)
4. **Preserve all existing cluster config**: private_cluster_config, release_channel, workload_identity_config, maintenance_policy, master_authorized_networks_config, ip_allocation_policy, resource_labels — keep all of these unchanged.
5. Add `google_container_node_pool.default` resource with:
   - `machine_type` from variable (default `e2-small`)
   - `spot` from variable (default `true`)
   - `autoscaling { min_node_count` from variable (default `0`), `max_node_count` from variable (default `3`) }
   - `oauth_scopes = ["https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform"]`
   - `workload_metadata_config { mode = "GKE_METADATA" }` (for Workload Identity)
   - `management { auto_repair = true, auto_upgrade = true }`
6. Add `google_container_node_pool.executor` resource with same structure as default, plus:
   - `machine_type` from variable (default `e2-medium`)
   - `taint { key = "executor-only", value = "true", effect = "NO_SCHEDULE" }`
   - Separate min/max variables (default min `0`, max `5`)
7. Add new variables with types and descriptions: `zone` (string), `default_pool_machine_type` (string, default "e2-small"), `default_pool_min_nodes` (number, default 0), `default_pool_max_nodes` (number, default 3), `default_pool_spot` (bool, default true), `executor_pool_machine_type` (string, default "e2-medium"), `executor_pool_min_nodes` (number, default 0), `executor_pool_max_nodes` (number, default 5), `executor_pool_spot` (bool, default true)
8. Keep `var.region` (still needed by other modules). Add `var.zone` for cluster location. Change cluster `location` to `var.zone`.
9. Update all outputs to reference `google_container_cluster.main` instead of `.autopilot`
10. Update module header comment to reflect Standard mode

## Notes
- No special node pool boot/security config needed for executor capabilities — GKE Standard allows SYS_ADMIN and procMount: Unmasked by default (unlike Autopilot)
- The region variable is preserved since other modules (Cloud SQL, VPC) still need it

title: Replace Memorystore Redis with in-cluster Redis
description:
## Summary
Replace the managed Memorystore Redis (~$37/mo) with an in-cluster Redis pod that scales to zero with the node pool. Centrifugo uses Redis for ephemeral presence/history data that rebuilds naturally on restart.

## Files to modify
- `k8s/base/redis-deployment.yaml` (new) — Redis Deployment
- `k8s/base/redis-service.yaml` (new) — Redis ClusterIP Service
- `k8s/base/kustomization.yaml` — Add redis resources
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/main.tf` — Remove module "redis" block (lines ~162-175), update Centrifugo module redis_host/redis_port
- `infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/variables.tf` — Remove redis variables (lines ~181-193)
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/centrifugo/main.tf` — Update redis_address reference
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/centrifugo/variables.tf` — Simplify redis config variables

## Implementation steps
1. Create `k8s/base/redis-deployment.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   apiVersion: apps/v1
   kind: Deployment
   metadata:
     name: redis
     labels:
       app: redis
   spec:
     replicas: 1
     selector:
       matchLabels:
         app: redis
     template:
       metadata:
         labels:
           app: redis
       spec:
         containers:
           - name: redis
             image: redis:7-alpine
             ports:
               - containerPort: 6379
             resources:
               requests:
                 memory: 64Mi
                 cpu: 50m
               limits:
                 memory: 256Mi
   ```
2. Create `k8s/base/redis-service.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   apiVersion: v1
   kind: Service
   metadata:
     name: redis
   spec:
     type: ClusterIP
     selector:
       app: redis
     ports:
       - port: 6379
         targetPort: 6379
   ```
3. Add `redis-deployment.yaml` and `redis-service.yaml` to `k8s/base/kustomization.yaml` resources list
4. In prod main.tf: remove `module "redis"` block entirely
5. In prod main.tf: update `module "centrifugo"` to pass `redis_host = "redis.default.svc.cluster.local"` and `redis_port = 6379` as literals instead of `module.redis.host`/`module.redis.port`
6. In prod variables.tf: remove `redis_tier` and `redis_memory_size_gb` variables
7. Keep `infrastructure/terraform/modules/redis/` directory intact (useful if a future environment needs managed Redis)

## Notes
- Redis data is ephemeral (presence, 10-message history with 5min TTL). Loss on restart is acceptable.
- Centrifugo clients auto-reconnect and presence rebuilds from periodic pings.

title: Change executor ScaledObject minReplicaCount to 0 for scale-to-zero
description:
## Summary
The executor ScaledObject has minReplicaCount: 1, which keeps at least one executor pod running at all times. This prevents the executor node pool from scaling to zero. Change to minReplicaCount: 0 so the entire executor pool can scale down when idle.

## Files to modify
- `k8s/base/executor-scaledobject.yaml` (line 8)

## Before → After
Before:
```yaml
  minReplicaCount: 1
```

After:
```yaml
  minReplicaCount: 0
```

## Notes
- With minReplicaCount: 0, there will be a cold start delay when the first execution request arrives (node pool scale-up + pod scheduling). This is acceptable for development.
- For production, change back to minReplicaCount: 1 to eliminate cold starts.

title: Add handler-level error logging for 500 responses
Replace NoRLSStoreMiddleware with limited registration role
description:
## Summary

The `NoRLSStoreMiddleware` bypasses RLS entirely for registration routes, giving handlers unrestricted database access. This is a security concern - we should instead use a limited PostgreSQL role with specific RLS policies.

## Current Problem

Registration routes (`/auth/accept-invite`, `/auth/register-student`) need database access before a user exists. Currently, `NoRLSStoreMiddleware` provides a Store without any RLS context, meaning handlers have full access to all tables.

## Solution

Create RLS policies for a 'registration' context that only allow the specific operations needed:

### Exact Permissions

| Table | SELECT | INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE |
|-------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| invitations | pending only | ❌ | pending→accepted | ❌ |
| sections | active only | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| classes | ✅ (via section) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| users | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| section_memberships | ❌ | student role only | ❌ | ❌ |
| namespaces | active only | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |

### Implementation

1. Add migration with helper function and RLS policies:

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION is_registration_context() RETURNS boolean AS $$
BEGIN
  RETURN current_setting('app.role', true) = 'registration';
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql STABLE;

-- invitations: SELECT pending, UPDATE pending→accepted
CREATE POLICY "invitations_registration_select" ON invitations
  FOR SELECT USING (is_registration_context() AND status = 'pending');

CREATE POLICY "invitations_registration_update" ON invitations
  FOR UPDATE USING (is_registration_context() AND status = 'pending')
  WITH CHECK (status = 'accepted');

-- sections: SELECT active only
CREATE POLICY "sections_registration_select" ON sections
  FOR SELECT USING (is_registration_context() AND active = true);

-- classes: SELECT (needed for join code info)
CREATE POLICY "classes_registration_select" ON classes
  FOR SELECT USING (is_registration_context());

-- users: INSERT only
CREATE POLICY "users_registration_insert" ON users
  FOR INSERT WITH CHECK (is_registration_context());

-- memberships: INSERT student role only
CREATE POLICY "memberships_registration_insert" ON section_memberships
  FOR INSERT WITH CHECK (is_registration_context() AND role = 'student');

-- namespaces: SELECT active only (needed for FK)
CREATE POLICY "namespaces_registration_select" ON namespaces
  FOR SELECT USING (is_registration_context() AND active = true);
```

2. Rename `NoRLSStoreMiddleware` → `RegistrationStoreMiddleware`

3. Update middleware to set `app.role = 'registration'` instead of bypassing RLS

## Files to Modify

- `migrations/00X_registration_rls.up.sql` (new)
- `migrations/00X_registration_rls.down.sql` (new)
- `go-backend/internal/middleware/rls.go`
- `go-backend/internal/server/server.go`

## Security Benefit

- RLS is still enforced, just with limited policies
- Registration can only access what it needs
- Cannot read existing users, problems, sessions, revisions, etc.
- Audit trail maintained

title: ElastiCache Redis Cluster
description:
## Summary
Provision ElastiCache Redis for caching, rate limiting, and Centrifugo pub/sub backend.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- ElastiCache Redis cluster (cluster mode disabled for simplicity)
- Subnet group (database subnets)
- Security group (allow access from EKS only)
- Parameter group with appropriate settings

## Configuration
- Node type: cache.t3.micro (staging), cache.r6g.large (prod)
- Replicas: 0 for staging, 2 for prod (Multi-AZ)
- Encryption: In-transit and at-rest
- Eviction policy: volatile-lru

## Use Cases
- Centrifugo pub/sub backend (channel presence, message routing)
- Rate limiting (sliding window counters)
- Session caching (optional, JWTs are stateless)
- API response caching (optional)

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/elasticache/`
- Outputs: Primary endpoint, reader endpoint, port

## Dependencies
- Requires: VPC & Networking Foundation (PLAT-03e)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for caching strategy

Configure Resend SMTP for Identity Platform
description:
## Summary
Configure Identity Platform to use Resend for sending verification and password reset emails.

## Prerequisites
- Resend account configured
- Domain verified in Resend
- API key available

## Configuration Steps

### 1. Get Resend SMTP Credentials
From Resend dashboard:
- SMTP Host: `smtp.resend.com`
- Port: `465` (SSL) or `587` (TLS)
- Username: `resend`
- Password: Your Resend API key

### 2. Configure in GCP Console
1. Go to: https://console.cloud.google.com/customer-identity/settings
2. Navigate to "Email templates" or "SMTP settings"
3. Enable custom SMTP
4. Enter Resend SMTP credentials
5. Set sender address (must match verified domain in Resend)

### 3. Verify Configuration
- Send test verification email
- Check Resend dashboard for delivery status
- Verify email arrives with correct sender

## Terraform (if supported)
```hcl
# Note: SMTP config may require manual setup in console
# Document the configuration for reproducibility
```

## Done When
- [ ] Resend SMTP configured in Identity Platform
- [ ] Test email sends successfully
- [ ] Sender shows custom domain (not firebaseapp.com)
- [ ] Configuration documented for other environments
close_reason: Resend SMTP configured in Firebase Console. Test email sent successfully.

title: Typed API client layer
description:
Frontend hooks call generic apiGet<T>('/path') where T is whatever the developer guesses. Hook unit tests mock responses with wrong shapes (e.g., { classes: [...] } when the Go API returns [...]). Contract tests validate the real shapes but share zero code with hooks or unit tests. Result: systematic response-format bugs that only surface in E2E tests.

Solution: Create typed API functions (one per endpoint) in lib/api/ that hooks call instead of raw apiGet<T>. Contract tests call the same typed functions against the real API. Hook unit tests mock the typed module — mocks are type-safe by construction.

Scope: All 8 hooks (~35 API calls), their unit/integration tests, contract tests.
Out of scope: ~70 direct apiFetch calls in components/pages (follow-up epic PLAT-7vg).

See plan: .claude/plans/serialized-noodling-dijkstra.md

title: Typed API client: foundation + classes
description:
Create lib/api/ module structure and lib/api/classes.ts with 9 typed functions. This subtask establishes the pattern for all other subtasks.

## Implementation steps

1. Create frontend/src/lib/api/classes.ts with typed functions:
   - listClasses(): Promise<Class[]> — GET /classes
   - createClass(name, description?): Promise<Class> — POST /classes
   - updateClass(id, updates): Promise<Class> — PATCH /classes/{id}
   - deleteClass(id): Promise<void> — DELETE /classes/{id}
   - createSection(classId, name, semester?): Promise<Section> — POST /classes/{classId}/sections
   - updateSection(sectionId, updates): Promise<Section> — PATCH /sections/{sectionId}
   - regenerateJoinCode(sectionId): Promise<Section> — POST /sections/{sectionId}/regenerate-code
   - addCoInstructor(sectionId, email): Promise<void> — POST /sections/{sectionId}/instructors
   - removeCoInstructor(sectionId, userId): Promise<void> — DELETE /sections/{sectionId}/instructors/{userId}

2. Create frontend/src/lib/api/index.ts re-exporting all modules.

3. Migrate frontend/src/hooks/useClasses.ts to import and call typed functions instead of raw apiGet/apiPost/apiPatch/apiDelete.

4. Update hook unit tests to mock typed module with correct shapes:
   - hooks/__tests__/useClasses.test.ts — jest.mock('@/lib/api/classes'), mock returns plain arrays/objects (not wrapped)
   - hooks/__tests__/useClasses.integration.test.ts — fix mock fetch shapes from { classes: [...] } to [...]
   - hooks/__tests__/useClasses-logic.integration.test.ts — same fix

5. Update contract test infrastructure for typed function calls:
   - __tests__/contract/helpers.ts — add setupTestAuth() that calls setTestUser() from @/lib/test-auth
   - __tests__/contract/globalSetup.ts — set NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL and NEXT_PUBLIC_AUTH_MODE env vars

6. Update __tests__/contract/classes.integration.test.ts to call listClasses() directly instead of contractFetch('/api/v1/classes').

## Key response shape corrections
- GET /classes: { classes: Class[] } → Class[] (plain array)
- POST /classes: { class: Class } → Class (direct object)
- PATCH /classes/{id}: { class: Class } → Class
- POST /classes/{classId}/sections: { section: Section } → Section
- PATCH /sections/{id}: { section: Section } → Section
- POST /sections/{id}/regenerate-code: { join_code: string } → Section (full object)

## Files to modify
- frontend/src/lib/api/classes.ts (new)
- frontend/src/lib/api/index.ts (new)
- frontend/src/hooks/useClasses.ts
- frontend/src/hooks/__tests__/useClasses.test.ts
- frontend/src/hooks/__tests__/useClasses.integration.test.ts
- frontend/src/hooks/__tests__/useClasses-logic.integration.test.ts
- frontend/src/__tests__/contract/classes.integration.test.ts
- frontend/src/__tests__/contract/helpers.ts
- frontend/src/__tests__/contract/globalSetup.ts

title: Add named port to go-api deployment for PodMonitoring clarity
Rate limiting on auth routes
description:
Auth endpoints are public attack surface. Need brute-force protection.

Redis-based sliding window rate limiter (already have Redis for Centrifugo):
- Auth routes (login, register): 5 req/min per IP
- Join-code validation: 10 req/min per IP
- Middleware that wraps specific route groups
- Graceful degradation if Redis unavailable

Note: Token revocation (Redis blocklist) should be implemented alongside user management endpoints (PLAT-vyf.10). When a user is deleted/deactivated, add their subject to a Redis blocklist with TTL matching token expiry (~1h). No separate issue — Redis infrastructure comes from this work.

Files:
- go-backend/internal/middleware/ratelimit.go — rate limit middleware
- go-backend/internal/server/server.go — apply to auth route group

title: Fix type mismatches in migrated components
description:
## Summary
After migrating components from direct apiFetch calls to the typed API client (PLAT-7vg),
there are type mismatches between local component interfaces and the canonical API types.

## Root Cause
Components define local interfaces (e.g., local User, Invitation, ClassInfo types) that use
`| undefined` for optional fields, while the API types use `| null`. Also, some local types
expect additional computed properties that the API doesn't return.

## Files Affected
- frontend/src/app/(app)/admin/page.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/classes/components/SectionCard.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/ClassList.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/InstructorDashboard.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/ProblemCreator.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/ProblemLibrary.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/SectionView.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/SessionsList.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/namespace/invitations/page.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/sections/[sectionId]/page.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(app)/system/page.tsx
- frontend/src/app/(projector)/public-view/page.tsx
- frontend/src/components/NamespaceHeader.tsx

## Implementation Steps
1. For each file, either:
   a. Import the canonical type from @/types/api or @/lib/api/* and remove the local definition
   b. Update the local interface to match the API type (null instead of undefined)
2. Handle cases where components expect computed properties (e.g., section_count) by either:
   a. Updating the API to return these properties
   b. Computing them client-side from the returned data
3. Run TypeScript and tests after each file to verify compatibility

## Test Command
cd frontend && npx tsc --noEmit
close_reason: Stale: tsc --noEmit compiles clean, all files already use canonical API types

title: Executor timeout detection is unreliable and has no Go-level deadline
description:
## Summary
Timeout detection uses a fragile wall-clock heuristic that can misclassify non-timeout failures. Also, no Go-level context deadline is set on the exec.Command, so if nsjail hangs, the sandbox.Run blocks indefinitely.

## Files to modify
- `executor/internal/sandbox/sandbox.go` (lines 168-171, and around line 140)

## Implementation
1. Pass a context with timeout to exec.CommandContext (e.g., timeoutSec + small buffer for nsjail overhead)
2. Detect timeout via context.DeadlineExceeded rather than wall-clock heuristic
3. Keep exit code 137 as a secondary signal
4. Add tests for the improved timeout detection
close_reason: Implemented: context deadline replaces wall-clock heuristic

title: Port Playwright E2E tests from old repo
description:
## Summary
Port Playwright E2E tests from old repo to this project. Tests cover system-admin, critical user paths (instructor+student), and public view flows.

## Current Status: BLOCKED on PLAT-817

Work paused to fix systematic frontend response-shape mismatches (PLAT-817: typed API client layer). Once that PR merges and this branch is rebased, resume debugging.

## Branch & Worktree
- Branch: `feature/e2e-tests` (PR #38)
- Worktree: `/workspaces/eval-e2e-tests`
- Worktree has LOCAL MODIFICATIONS to 2 contract test files (stale edits, can discard with `git checkout -- .`)

## What's Done
- E2E test infrastructure: Playwright config, Docker Compose, test fixtures, API helpers
- 3 test specs ported: system-admin.spec.ts, critical-paths.spec.ts, public-view.spec.ts
- Console log capture on test failure (test-fixture.ts)
- Manual hook response-shape fixes in E2E worktree (will be SUPERSEDED by PLAT-817)
- Namespace detail page fix (will be SUPERSEDED by PLAT-817.2)

## What Remains After PLAT-817 Merges

### 1. Rebase and discard superseded changes
The E2E worktree has manual hook fixes that PLAT-817 replaces properly. After rebasing:
- Discard hook changes (useClasses, useNamespaces, useSections, useInvitations, useSessionHistory)
- Discard namespace detail page fix (covered by PLAT-817.2)
- Keep: test infrastructure, spec files, console log capture

### 2. Re-verify system-admin test
The system-admin test navigated successfully to the namespace detail page but failed finding "Test Organization" text. Root cause was the response-shape bug in `system/namespaces/[id]/page.tsx` which PLAT-817.2 fixes. Should pass after rebase.

### 3. Debug instructor blank-page bug (MAIN REMAINING WORK)
3 tests fail with blank white page after instructor login:
- critical-paths test 1: "Complete workflow: Instructor setup and student participation"
- critical-paths test 2: "Student code sync: code changes sync to instructor and public view"
- public-view: "Public view updates when instructor features different students"

All fail at the same point: after instructor signs in via test auth, the page shows blank white (or redirects back to login). The locator `h2:has-text("Dashboard"), button:has-text("Create Your First Class")` never appears.

This is NOT a timeout issue — the page is genuinely blank. Likely causes to investigate:
- Auth context not propagating to the instructor's page (cookie/localStorage not set?)
- The instructor user may not have the right role/namespace setup for the dashboard to render
- A component-level apiFetch call (not covered by PLAT-817 hooks) may be crashing silently
- Check browser console logs (now captured by test-fixture.ts) for errors

### 4. Screenshots from last run
- system-admin: `test-results/system-admin-System-Admin--52384-pace-and-view-invitation-UI-chromium/`
- critical-paths: `test-results/critical-paths-Critical-Us-0470a--instructor-and-public-view-chromium/`
- public-view: `test-results/public-view-Public-View-Fe-c3000-features-different-students-chromium/`

- PLAT-817 (typed API client) — fixes response-shape bugs that affect E2E tests

title: Playwright infrastructure + frontend test auth mode
description:
## Summary
Set up Playwright test infrastructure and a frontend test auth mode that pairs with the backend's existing AUTH_MODE=test. This is the foundation for all E2E tests.

## Backend (already exists)
- `AUTH_MODE=test` accepts tokens in format `test:<external_id>:<email>` (go-backend/internal/auth/testvalidator.go)
- JWT middleware still runs — requests without auth headers get 401
- Contract tests already use this pattern (frontend/src/__tests__/contract/helpers.ts)

## Frontend changes needed

### New: `frontend/src/lib/test-auth.ts`
- `setTestUser(externalId, email)` / `getTestToken()` / `clearTestUser()`
- Module-level state, used by AuthContext and api-client

### Modify: `frontend/src/contexts/AuthContext.tsx`
- When `NEXT_PUBLIC_AUTH_MODE=test`: export a TestAuthProvider instead of AuthProvider
- TestAuthProvider.signIn(email, password) derives externalId from email, stores via test-auth module, calls GET /auth/me with test token
- TestAuthProvider.signOut() clears test user
- No Firebase SDK calls in test mode

### Modify: `frontend/src/lib/api-client.ts`
- getAuthHeaders(): when NEXT_PUBLIC_AUTH_MODE=test, return `Bearer test:<id>:<email>` from test-auth module
- Otherwise: existing Firebase path unchanged

## Playwright setup

### New: `frontend/playwright.config.ts`
- Based on old repo's config (/workspaces/coding-tool/playwright.config.ts)
- webServer array: Go backend (AUTH_MODE=test) + Next.js (NEXT_PUBLIC_AUTH_MODE=test)
- Chromium only, 30s timeout, HTML reporter

### New: `frontend/e2e/fixtures/auth.ts`
- signInAs(page, role, externalId?, namespace?) — navigates to /auth/signin, fills form
- loginAsInstructor(), loginAsStudent(), loginAsSystemAdmin() convenience wrappers

### New: `frontend/e2e/fixtures/api-setup.ts`
- Direct HTTP helpers reusing contract test patterns
- testToken(), createNamespace(), createClass(), createSection(), startSession()
- cleanupNamespace() for teardown

### New: `frontend/e2e/fixtures/test-fixture.ts`
- Extended Playwright test with per-test namespace isolation + cleanup

### New: `scripts/run-e2e-tests.sh`
- Starts postgres (reuse ensure-test-postgres.sh), Go server, Next.js
- Runs playwright test, cleanup on exit

### Modify: `Makefile`
- Add test-e2e target

## Patterns to follow
- frontend/src/__tests__/contract/globalSetup.ts — API data setup via test tokens
- frontend/src/__tests__/contract/helpers.ts — testToken(), contractFetch()
- scripts/run-contract-tests.sh — launch script pattern

title: Go API Health & Observability
description:
## Summary
Implement health checks, structured logging, and metrics for the Go API to enable monitoring and debugging.

## Scope
- Health check endpoints (liveness, readiness)
- Structured JSON logging with request context
- Prometheus metrics endpoint
- Request tracing with correlation IDs
- Error tracking integration (optional: Sentry)

## Health Endpoints
\`\`\`
GET /healthz        # Liveness - always 200 if process is running
GET /readyz         # Readiness - checks DB, Redis, dependencies
GET /readyz?verbose # Detailed component status
\`\`\`

Readiness checks:
- Database connection pool health
- Redis connectivity
- Cognito JWKS reachable (cached)

## Structured Logging
\`\`\`go
// Using slog (Go 1.21+ stdlib)
slog.Info("request completed",
    "method", r.Method,
    "path", r.URL.Path,
    "status", status,
    "duration_ms", duration.Milliseconds(),
    "request_id", middleware.GetReqID(r.Context()),
    "user_id", user.ID,
)
\`\`\`

## Metrics
Prometheus metrics at `/metrics`:
- `http_requests_total{method, path, status}` - request counter
- `http_request_duration_seconds{method, path}` - latency histogram
- `db_pool_connections{state}` - connection pool gauge
- `db_query_duration_seconds` - query latency histogram

## Request Tracing
- Generate request ID if not present (X-Request-ID header)
- Propagate through all logs and downstream calls
- Include in error responses for debugging

## Deliverables
- \`internal/handler/health.go\` - health endpoints
- \`internal/middleware/logging.go\` - request logging
- \`internal/middleware/metrics.go\` - Prometheus instrumentation
- \`internal/middleware/requestid.go\` - correlation ID handling
- Grafana dashboard JSON (optional)

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for observability requirements
- Prometheus Go client: github.com/prometheus/client_golang
close_reason: Merged: feat: Go API Health & Observability (#12)

title: Add status query param validation on sessions List endpoint
Registration page sends unauthenticated requests to JWT-protected endpoints
description:
## Summary
The student registration page and accept-invite page both use `publicFetchRaw` (no Authorization header) to call Go backend endpoints that require JWT authentication. These endpoints will return 401 for real users.

## Root cause
All `/api/v1` routes are behind `jwtValidator.Validate` middleware (go-backend/internal/server/server.go:125). The registration routes (server.go:128-138) use `RegistrationStoreMiddleware` for RLS but still require a valid JWT for identity.

## Affected files
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/register/student/page.tsx` (lines 243, 188)
  - `publicFetchRaw('/auth/register-student', { method: 'POST', ... })` — no auth header
  - `publicFetchRaw('/auth/register-student?code=...')` — GET also behind JWT middleware
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/invite/accept/page.tsx` (lines 136, 211)
  - `publicFetchRaw('/auth/accept-invite?...')` — no auth header
  - `publicFetchRaw('/auth/accept-invite', { method: 'POST', ... })` — no auth header

## Expected production flow
1. Student/invitee creates a Firebase Auth account client-side (createUserWithEmailAndPassword)
2. Firebase returns a JWT (Identity Platform token)
3. Frontend sends the JWT with the registration/accept-invite API call
4. Go backend validates JWT, extracts external_id and email from claims
5. Backend creates user_profiles record

## Fix approach
The registration and accept-invite pages need to:
1. Create the Firebase Auth account first (Step 1 of the flow)
2. Wait for the auth state to update (onAuthStateChanged fires)
3. Use `apiFetch` (authenticated) instead of `publicFetchRaw` for the POST
4. The GET endpoints for code validation / invite lookup may need to be moved outside the JWT group, OR the user creates the Firebase account before validation

## Notes
- The Go handler correctly extracts identity from JWT claims, not the request body (security)
- The backend side is correct; only the frontend calls need updating
- This blocks real user registration and invite acceptance

title: Fix duplicate realtime event emission on idempotent session updates
description:
## Summary
The session update handler (sessions.go:172-180) publishes realtime events based on request payload fields rather than comparing against the previous state. This means idempotent re-sends of the same PATCH request will emit duplicate session_ended or featured_student_changed events, potentially causing UI flicker on connected clients.

## Files to modify
- go-backend/internal/handler/sessions.go (lines ~172-180)

## Implementation
Compare the request values against the session's current state before publishing events:
1. For session_ended: only publish if session.Status != "completed" AND req.Status == "completed"
2. For featured_student_changed: only publish if the featured student actually changed (compare against current session.FeaturedStudentID)

title: Accept-invite page uses Supabase token format instead of invitation UUID
description:
## Summary
The accept-invite page reads Supabase-style hash tokens from the URL (`#token_hash=...&type=invite`) but the Go backend expects a simple `?token=<uuid>` query parameter for invitation lookup.

## Affected files
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/invite/accept/page.tsx` (lines 109-126)
  - Reads `locationHash`, parses `token_hash` and `access_token` from URL fragment
  - Requires `type=invite` or `type=magiclink`
  - Sends to Go backend as query params: `?token_hash=...&access_token=...&type=...`
- `go-backend/internal/handler/auth.go` — GetAcceptInvite handler
  - Expects `?token=<uuid>` query parameter (invitation ID)

## Current behavior
1. Invitation email contains link with Supabase-style hash: `/invite/accept#token_hash=<token>&type=invite`
2. Frontend parses hash, sends `GET /auth/accept-invite?token_hash=<token>&type=invite`
3. Go backend looks for `?token=` param, doesn't find it → returns error

## Fix approach
Update the accept-invite page to support the Go backend's URL format:
- Support `?token=<invitation_uuid>` query parameter
- The invitation handler in the Go backend uses the invitation UUID directly
- Update invitation email template (Resend) to generate links in the new format: `/invite/accept?token=<invitation_uuid>`
- Can keep backward compatibility with hash format if needed during transition

## Related
- go-backend/internal/handler/invitations.go — generates acceptURL with invitation ID
- go-backend/internal/email/resend.go — sends invitation emails with acceptURL

title: Add error detail logging to HTTP handlers - 500s only log status code, not underlying error
description:
## Summary
The Go API request logging middleware only logs HTTP method, path, status code, and duration. When handlers return 500, the actual error (e.g., DB errors, RLS violations) is not logged, making production debugging impossible.

## Current behavior
```
level=INFO msg="http request" method=POST path=/api/v1/auth/bootstrap status=500 duration_ms=3.04
```

## Expected behavior
500-level responses should log the error message/cause, e.g.:
```
level=ERROR msg="http request" method=POST path=/api/v1/auth/bootstrap status=500 duration_ms=3.04 error="pq: new row violates row-level security policy for table users"
```

## Files to modify
- go-backend/internal/handler/auth.go - pass errors to a logging-aware error writer
- go-backend/internal/middleware/ - enhance request logging to capture error context
- go-backend/internal/httputil/ - consider adding error context to WriteError

## Approach
Either thread errors through the response writer wrapper so the logging middleware can capture them, or log at the handler level before calling WriteError.
close_reason: WriteError captures error detail on response writer for 5xx; logging middleware reads it back at ERROR level. Tests added.

title: Replace Repos god interface with narrower per-handler interfaces
description:
The store.Repos interface embeds all 13 repository interfaces (~60 methods). Every handler gets access to everything even when it only needs 2-3 methods. This is a god interface anti-pattern that obscures actual dependencies.

Options: (a) typed context helpers like store.Users(ctx) returning UserRepository; (b) per-handler composite interfaces; (c) keep Repos but add handler-level dep documentation via comments.
